heading: MODULO TREDICESIMO
card_summary: This module, forming part of the Tono Ottavo (Eighth Tone) within the Cantico Terzo, develops an extended theology of Christ's triumph using the language of Roman military victory (triumphus) combined with kabbalistic and Neoplatonic symbolism. Zorzi argues that Christ's warfare against Satan was a iustum bellum in the classical sense, formally declared through the prophets as fetials, and that his solitary victory — prefigured in Isaiah's winepress image — earned a full triumphal procession at the Ascension. The triumph is allegorized through the four properties of the glorified body as four white horses, and the kabbalistic sefirah Tif'eret is identified as Christ's sanctuary and the meaning of the Hebrew name of divine glory/triumph. The apostles receive vicarious triumphs over each of their twelve mission-territories, presiding over the twelve tribes of Israel, while the triple enemy of the dragon (concupiscence of the flesh, of the eyes, and pride of life) is set against the Blessed Trinity in a precise typological scheme. Sources include Isaiah, Zechariah, Revelation (John), Paul's letters to the Colossians and Ephesians, Pseudo-Dionysius, and Ambrose's De Patriarchis.
essay:
## Overview and Structure

Chunk 126 presents the Modulo Tredicesimo of the Tono Ottavo within the third Cantico of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. Its subject is the *triumphus* of Christ: the text is organized around four *concentus* (harmonic sub-movements) that move from the conditions for a just war, through Christ's solitary victory and his triumphal Ascension, to the apostolic triumphs conducted in Christ's name across the known world. The organizing metaphor is Roman military triumph, but every element is systematically theologized, kabbalized, and harmonized with the larger cosmological project.

---

## 1. The Just War (Concentus I): Christ as Rightful Commander

Zorzi opens by applying the classical criteria of *iustum bellum* to Christ's struggle against Satan. A legitimate battle requires: a just cause, a formal declaration of war, princely authority, and the absence of personal vendetta. Zorzi establishes each criterion:

> *È del tutto evidente che il nostro Comandante Cristo aveva una giusta causa nella guerra che ha scatenato contro il nemico del genere umano poiché quel serpente antico, come un crudelissimo predone teneva prigionieri gli uomini sotto un durissimo e insopportabile giogo di schiavitù.*

<cite>p. 2671–2672</cite>

The declaration of war was issued through the prophets, who function as *feziali* (the Roman priestly heralds who formally declared war). Chief among them is the "propheta regalis" (the Psalmist), citing Psalm 71: "Ha liberato il povero dal potente." The Father's mandate for Christ's war is drawn from Isaiah 53 ("A causa dei peccati del mio popolo lo ho colpito").

The battle against the dragon involves a structural typology: Michael and his angels war against the dragon following the same rules as Christ himself — obedience vs. contumacy, love vs. hatred, zeal vs. envy, piety vs. pride. Apocalypse 12 (the woman clothed with the sun, the twelve stars, the ancient serpent) is read as a literal cosmic drama in which the woman is the Virgin, the twelve stars are the apostolic twelve, and the moon under her feet is the Synagogue.

The three-headed beast that the dragon unleashes after his defeat — concupiscence of the flesh, concupiscence of the eyes, pride of life (1 John 2:16) — corresponds precisely to the three persons of the evil triad (Azazel for carnal temptation, Asmodeo for pride/arrogance), set against the Blessed Trinity:

> *Questa battaglia triplice, cioè in spirito, nella dimensione sensibile dell'uomo e nell'attrattiva esercitata su di noi dai beni esteriori che ci circondano, è stata ingaggiata perché, dopo aver sconfitto in tre scontri la bestia a tre teste, o almeno aver ben meritato nello scontro, possiamo raggiungere la Triade eccelsa.*

<cite>p. 2675</cite>

The three aspects of spiritual combat (against spirito, senso, and external pleasures) correspond to union with the three Persons: the Spirit requires the stripping of spiritual pride; the Son requires the crucifixion of carnal desire; the Father requires the total renunciation of temporal goods in *pietas* and *ardore*.

---

## 2. The Solitary Victory (Concentus II): Isaiah's Winepress and Paul's Chirographum

Zorzi presents Christ's victory as necessarily solitary. When Peter drew his sword at Gethsemane, Christ refused help; the cry of dereliction ("Deus meus, Deus meus ut quid dereliquisti me?") is interpreted as Christ's acknowledgment that he was alone in his duel against the most ferocious enemy. The typological proof text is Isaiah 63:3: "Torcular calcavi solus, et de gentibus non est vir mecum."

The legal theology of redemption draws on Colossians 2:14–15 in Paul's technical language: the *chirographum decreti* — the bond or document contracted with the devil through sinful deeds — is annulled by being nailed to the Cross. Zorzi cites this passage in full (Latin and Italian), glossing "Expolians principatus et potestates traduxit confidenter, palam triumphans illos in se ipso" as a complete exposition of the logic of Christ's war.

Ambrose's *De Patriarchis* is cited as providing the fullest discussion of why Christ had to fight alone: so that humanity would be debtor to the Redeemer alone.

---

## 3. The Triumphal Ascension (Concentus III): Four White Horses and Tif'eret

The triumphal procession of the Ascension is elaborated in rich allegorical detail. Christ mounts the chariot of his glorified body, drawn by four white horses representing the four *dotes corporis gloriosi* (properties of the glorified body): agility (from heavy to light), subtlety (from gross to fine), incorruptibility, and clarity (from dark to luminous as at the Transfiguration).

The instruments of the Passion — cross, nails, lance — are the military standards (*insignia*) of the triumph. The Church hymn "Vexilla regis prodeunt, fulget crucis mysterium" is cited, as is "Regnavit dominus a ligno." The dragon is bound in chains (Revelation 20:1–2), though with a significant eschatological note: the thousand years will expire, and the Antichrist will then be permitted powers not seen since Christ, "quamquam praestigiosa erunt omnia" (but all will be illusions).

The Ascension Psalm (Psalm 46) is interpreted messiologically, including the Hebrew term for shofar:

> *Il Dio uomo è asceso nel giubilo, il suono più degno di tutti, il Signore al suono di tromba, ovvero di corno, che si suonava al tempo del giubileo ed è chiamato in ebraico* שׁוֹפָר, *shofar.*

<cite>p. 2513</cite>

The crucial kabbalistic identification in this section is between Christ's sanctuary-throne and the sefirah *Tif'eret*:

> *Questo santuario è detto nell'Archetipo* תִּפְאֶרֶת, *tif'eret, cioè gloria, bellezza e trionfo. Per questa ragione con un mistero figurale, è detto in Isaia: Come hai condotto il tuo popolo un tempo fuori dall'Egitto per opera di Mosè, per conquistarti il nome tif'eret, che la traduzione corrente rende con 'un nome glorioso', ma può anche essere inteso come 'un nome di trionfo'.*

<cite>p. 2513</cite>

This is a signature Zorzian move: the sefirah Tif'eret (the sixth sefirah, associated with the divine Son/Metatron in kabbalistic theology) is identified with Christ's glory, beauty, and triumph, and linked to the Exodus typology via Isaiah 63. Zechariah 9:11 ("in sanguine testamenti tui eduxisti vinctos de lacu") is adduced as prophetic confirmation that the blood of Christ replicated and surpassed the blood of the Passover covenant.

Pseudo-Dionysius is cited (without further specification) as the authority for reading Isaiah 63's dialogue between the angels and the returning Christ ("Quare rubrum est indumentum tuum sicut calcantium in torculari?") as a description of the triumphal Ascension.

---

## 4. Apostolic Triumphs (Concentus IV): The Twelve Princes and the New Israel

The final concentus extends the triumph to the apostles, who serve as subordinate commanders in Christ's cosmic campaign. Zorzi provides a detailed geography of apostolic conquest: Paul over Antioch, Seleucia, Rhodes, Perge, Pamphylia, Cilicia, Phrygia, Philippi, Macedonia, Corinth, Thessalonica, Athens, Ephesus, the Greek islands, Italy, and Spain; Peter over Rome; John over Asia; Thomas over India. They are assigned to rule the twelve tribes of the new Israel, which Zorzi identifies with the totality of redeemed humanity.

> *Poiché conseguirono la vittoria, meritatamente dovevano ottenere il trionfo e le corone insieme al Comandante ed essere fatti sedere come suoi attendenti sui dodici troni per presiedere a giudicare le autentiche dodici tribù di Israele.*

<cite>p. 2516</cite>

The Psalmist's "Principes populorum congregati sunt cum Deo Abraham" is interpreted as referring to the apostles gathered around the God of Abraham — with an excursus on Abraham's name-change as the founding of the new regenerated family under the Redeemer. The apostles are called "Dii fortes terrae" (mighty gods of the earth), united with Christ to such a degree that they participate in the eschatological judgment, wielding the two-edged sword of Revelation 19 alongside Christ.

The section closes with Revelation 3:21 — "Chi vince, lo farò sedere sul mio trono" — as Christ's promise that the triumphus is available to all the saints, not just the apostles: "Gloria haec est omnibus sanctis eius."

---

## Cosmological and Kabbalistic Connections

This chunk advances Zorzi's persistent project of mapping Christian soteriology onto the kabbalistic sefirotic tree. The identification of Christ's sanctuary with Tif'eret is the doctrinal center of the module. In kabbalistic tradition, Tif'eret is the sefirah of beauty and divine harmony, often associated with the divine son or the heavenly Adam — Zorzi reads it as the very throne-name of the victorious Christ. The Exodus typology (Egypt = diabolical servitude; the blood of the covenant = Christ's blood) translates the historical salvation event into an archetypal pattern replicated in each soul's triple combat against the three-headed beast.

The structural use of the number twelve — twelve apostles, twelve tribes, twelve parts of the world — coheres with Zorzi's broader harmonic numerology, where the duodenarius is repeatedly described as the "divino, celeste e apostolico" number, the organizational principle of redeemed humanity.
